input.prompt:
Build a Blazor page for a multi-step job application form. This has many
distinct visual sections and shared state. Here are the service contracts already registered in DI:

```csharp
public record JobApplication(PersonalInfo Personal, WorkHistory Work, List<Reference> References, string CoverLetter);
public record PersonalInfo(string FullName, string Email, string Phone, string Address);
public record WorkHistory(List<WorkEntry> Entries);
public record WorkEntry(string Company, string Title, DateTime StartDate, DateTime? EndDate, string Description);
public record Reference(string Name, string Relationship, string Email, string Phone);

public interface IApplicationService
{
    Task<int> SubmitApplicationAsync(JobApplication application);
    Task<bool> ValidateEmailAsync(string email);
}
```

The page should have:

**Step indicator bar:**
- Shows all steps: "Personal Info" → "Work History" → "References" → "Cover Letter" → "Review & Submit"
- The current step is highlighted, completed steps show a checkmark, future steps are dimmed
- Users can click a completed step to go back and edit it

**Step 1 — Personal Info:**
- Inputs for full name, email, phone, and address
- Email is validated via ValidateEmailAsync when the user moves to the next step
- A "Next" button that validates required fields before advancing

**Step 2 — Work History:**
- A dynamic list of work entries — start with one empty entry
- Each entry has: company name, job title, start date, end date (optional, checkbox for "current"), description
- "Add Another Position" button to add more entries
- "Remove" button on each entry (except if it's the only one)
- "Back" and "Next" buttons

**Step 3 — References:**
- Space for 2-3 references, each with: name, relationship, email, phone
- "Add Reference" button (max 3)
- "Back" and "Next" buttons

**Step 4 — Cover Letter:**
- A large textarea for the cover letter
- A character count display (e.g., "342 / 2000 characters")
- "Back" and "Next" buttons

**Step 5 — Review & Submit:**
- Read-only summary of all entered data organized by section
- An "Edit" link next to each section that jumps back to that step
- A "Submit Application" button that calls SubmitApplicationAsync
- After submission, show a confirmation with the application ID

Route: `/apply`

  and data flow.
  DO NOT USE FOR: creating new Blazor projects or apps from scratch
  (use create-blazor-project), implementing a single individual component
  (use author-component), writing component code with parameters and
  EventCallback (use author-component), or simple single-component pages.
---

# Plan a Blazor UI Change

When asked to build a complex UI feature, **plan the component decomposition first, then immediately implement it**. A single monolithic page component is almost never the right answer — break the UI into focused, composable components.

## Planning Workflow

### Step 1 — Map the Visual Regions

Read the request and identify every distinct visual region. Each region that has its own data, behavior, or layout responsibility is a candidate component.

Draw the component tree:

```
InventoryDashboard          (page — owns data, orchestrates layout)
├── StockSummaryBar         (read-only stats: total items, low-stock count, value)
├── InventoryFilters        (search box, category dropdown, stock-level toggle)
├── InventoryTable          (sortable table of products)
│   └── InventoryRow        (single product row with inline edit/delete)
└── AddProductForm          (slide-out form for new products)
```

Rules for identifying components:
- **Distinct responsibility** — a region owns its own state or behavior → separate component
- **Repeated structure** — items in a list, cards in a grid → extract the item template
- **Independent interactivity** — a section that handles user input separately from its siblings → separate component
- **Size** — any section that would exceed ~150 lines of markup on its own → split it

### Step 2 — Classify Each Component

For every component in the tree, determine:

| Component | Action | Render Mode | State Owned | Lines (est.) |
|-----------|--------|-------------|-------------|-------------|
| InventoryDashboard | Create | InteractiveServer | product list, filter state | ~80 |
| StockSummaryBar | Create | (inherits) | none — receives data | ~30 |
| InventoryFilters | Create | (inherits) | search text, selected category | ~60 |
| InventoryTable | Create | (inherits) | sort column, sort direction | ~50 |
| InventoryRow | Create | (inherits) | inline-edit mode flag | ~60 |
| AddProductForm | Create | (inherits) | form model | ~80 |

**A page component that exceeds ~200 lines of combined markup + code is too large.** If your estimate puts a single component above that, split further.

### Step 3 — Design Data Flow

Identify the **state owner** for each piece of data, then map how it flows:

```
InventoryDashboard (owns: products[], filters)
  │
  ├─ [Parameter] products ──→ StockSummaryBar (reads aggregate stats)
  │
  ├─ [Parameter] filters ──→ InventoryFilters
  │   └─ EventCallback<Filters> OnFiltersChanged ──→ InventoryDashboard
  │
  ├─ [Parameter] filteredProducts ──→ InventoryTable
  │   └─ [Parameter] product ──→ InventoryRow
  │       ├─ EventCallback<Product> OnSave ──→ InventoryTable ──→ InventoryDashboard
  │       └─ EventCallback<Product> OnDelete ──→ InventoryTable ──→ InventoryDashboard
  │
  └─ EventCallback<Product> OnProductAdded ←── AddProductForm
```

Rules:
- Data always flows **down** through `[Parameter]`
- Events always flow **up** through `EventCallback<T>`
- The page/parent **owns the data** and passes filtered/transformed views to children
- Children **never mutate parameters** — they notify the parent via callbacks
- If data must cross more than 2 levels without intermediate components needing it, use a cascading value or a scoped service

### Step 4 — Identify Reuse Opportunities

Before creating a new component, check if an existing component in the project can serve the purpose. Look for:
- Existing list-item components that match the structure
- Shared filter/search components already in the project
- Generic components (e.g., `DataTable<T>`, `Pagination`) that accept templates

If a component will be used in more than one page, place it in a `Shared/` or `Components/` folder.

### Step 5 — Order the Implementation

Build bottom-up — leaf components first, then parents that compose them:

1. **Models/DTOs** — define the data shapes
2. **Services** — data access, business logic (interface + implementation)
3. **Leaf components** — components with no children (InventoryRow, StockSummaryBar)
4. **Container components** — components that compose leaves (InventoryTable, InventoryFilters)
5. **Page component** — wires everything together, registers routes
6. **Configuration** — DI registration, render mode setup

Each component should be independently compilable. Never reference a component that doesn't exist yet.

## Output Format

Present the plan briefly, then **immediately proceed to implement** — never stop at just the plan or ask for confirmation before writing code. The plan is a thinking tool, not a deliverable.

```markdown
## Component Plan: [Feature Name]

### Component Tree
[ASCII tree showing parent-child relationships]

### Component Table
| Component | Action | Render Mode | Purpose | Est. Lines |
|-----------|--------|-------------|---------|------------|
| ... | ... | ... | ... | ... |

### Data Flow
[State owner] → [Parameters down] → [EventCallbacks up]

### Implementation Order
1. [First file to create — why]
2. [Second file — why]
...
```

After outputting the plan, **immediately begin implementing** the components in the order listed. Do not wait for approval or ask "shall I proceed?" — the plan is a guide for you to follow, not a proposal for the user to approve.

## Anti-Patterns to Avoid

| Anti-Pattern | Why It's Wrong | Correct Approach |
|-------------|----------------|-----------------|
| One page component with 500+ lines | Impossible to test, reuse, or maintain | Decompose into focused components |
| Passing 10+ parameters through intermediate components | Parameter drilling obscures intent | Use cascading values or a scoped state service |
| Child component fetching its own data from an API | Multiple components making redundant calls | Parent owns data, passes via parameters |
| Inline rendering of list items with complex markup | Duplicated logic, no reuse, hard to test | Extract item template into its own component |
| Building everything in one file then "refactoring later" | Refactoring rarely happens; the monolith ships | Plan the decomposition upfront |
| Generic components for one-off usage | Over-engineering adds complexity | Only extract generics when reuse is proven |

## Guidelines

- **Plan briefly, then implement.** Write a concise component table and data flow map, then immediately create the `.razor` files — never stop at just the plan.
- **Prefer many small components over one large one.** A component with a single clear purpose is easier to understand, test, and reuse.
- **State ownership is the first decision.** Before writing fetch logic, decide which component owns the data.
- **Build bottom-up.** Create leaf components first so parent components can reference them immediately.
- **Name components after what they render**, not what they do internally: `ProductCard` not `ProductRenderer`, `OrderFilters` not `FilterHandler`.

  or multiple interacting sub-features, identifying parent-child relationships
  and data flow.
  DO NOT USE FOR: creating new Blazor projects or apps from scratch
  (use create-blazor-project), implementing a single individual component
  (use author-component), writing component code with parameters and
  EventCallback (use author-component), or simple single-component pages.
---

# Plan a Blazor UI Change

When asked to build a complex UI feature, **plan the component decomposition first, then immediately implement it**. A single monolithic page component is almost never the right answer — break the UI into focused, composable components.

## Planning Workflow

### Step 1 — Map the Visual Regions

Read the request and identify every distinct visual region. Each region that has its own data, behavior, or layout responsibility is a candidate component.

Draw the component tree:

```
InventoryDashboard          (page — owns data, orchestrates layout)
├── StockSummaryBar         (read-only stats: total items, low-stock count, value)
├── InventoryFilters        (search box, category dropdown, stock-level toggle)
├── InventoryTable          (sortable table of products)
│   └── InventoryRow        (single product row with inline edit/delete)
└── AddProductForm          (slide-out form for new products)
```

Rules for identifying components:
- **Distinct responsibility** — a region owns its own state or behavior → separate component
- **Repeated structure** — items in a list, cards in a grid → extract the item template
- **Independent interactivity** — a section that handles user input separately from its siblings → separate component
- **Size** — any section that would exceed ~150 lines of markup on its own → split it

### Step 2 — Classify Each Component

For every component in the tree, determine:

| Component | Action | Render Mode | State Owned | Lines (est.) |
|-----------|--------|-------------|-------------|-------------|
| InventoryDashboard | Create | InteractiveServer | product list, filter state | ~80 |
| StockSummaryBar | Create | (inherits) | none — receives data | ~30 |
| InventoryFilters | Create | (inherits) | search text, selected category | ~60 |
| InventoryTable | Create | (inherits) | sort column, sort direction | ~50 |
| InventoryRow | Create | (inherits) | inline-edit mode flag | ~60 |
| AddProductForm | Create | (inherits) | form model | ~80 |

**A page component that exceeds ~200 lines of combined markup + code is too large.** If your estimate puts a single component above that, split further.

### Step 3 — Design Data Flow

Identify the **state owner** for each piece of data, then map how it flows:

```
InventoryDashboard (owns: products[], filters)
  │
  ├─ [Parameter] products ──→ StockSummaryBar (reads aggregate stats)
  │
  ├─ [Parameter] filters ──→ InventoryFilters
  │   └─ EventCallback<Filters> OnFiltersChanged ──→ InventoryDashboard
  │
  ├─ [Parameter] filteredProducts ──→ InventoryTable
  │   └─ [Parameter] product ──→ InventoryRow
  │       ├─ EventCallback<Product> OnSave ──→ InventoryTable ──→ InventoryDashboard
  │       └─ EventCallback<Product> OnDelete ──→ InventoryTable ──→ InventoryDashboard
  │
  └─ EventCallback<Product> OnProductAdded ←── AddProductForm
```

Rules:
- Data always flows **down** through `[Parameter]`
- Events always flow **up** through `EventCallback<T>`
- The page/parent **owns the data** and passes filtered/transformed views to children
- Children **never mutate parameters** — they notify the parent via callbacks
- If data must cross more than 2 levels without intermediate components needing it, use a cascading value or a scoped service

### Step 4 — Identify Reuse Opportunities

Before creating a new component, check if an existing component in the project can serve the purpose. Look for:
- Existing list-item components that match the structure
- Shared filter/search components already in the project
- Generic components (e.g., `DataTable<T>`, `Pagination`) that accept templates

If a component will be used in more than one page, place it in a `Shared/` or `Components/` folder.

### Step 5 — Order the Implementation

Build bottom-up — leaf components first, then parents that compose them:

1. **Models/DTOs** — define the data shapes
2. **Services** — data access, business logic (interface + implementation)
3. **Leaf components** — components with no children (InventoryRow, StockSummaryBar)
4. **Container components** — components that compose leaves (InventoryTable, InventoryFilters)
5. **Page component** — wires everything together, registers routes
6. **Configuration** — DI registration, render mode setup

Each component should be independently compilable. Never reference a component that doesn't exist yet.

## Output Format

Present the plan briefly, then **immediately proceed to implement** — never stop at just the plan or ask for confirmation before writing code. The plan is a thinking tool, not a deliverable.

```markdown

|-----------|--------|-------------|---------|------------|
| ... | ... | ... | ... | ... |

2. [Second file — why]
...
```

| Inline rendering of list items with complex markup | Duplicated logic, no reuse, hard to test | Extract item template into its own component |
| Building everything in one file then "refactoring later" | Refactoring rarely happens; the monolith ships | Plan the decomposition upfront |
| Generic components for one-off usage | Over-engineering adds complexity | Only extract generics when reuse is proven |

data.reasoningText:
Let me plan the component decomposition for this multi-step job application form, then implement it.

## Component Plan: Job Application Form

### Component Tree
```
ApplyPage                    (page — owns all form state, orchestrates steps)
├── StepIndicator            (shows progress through 5 steps)
├── PersonalInfoStep         (step 1 — personal info form)
├── WorkHistoryStep          (step 2 — dynamic work entries)
│   └── WorkEntryForm        (single work entry)
├── ReferencesStep           (step 3 — references)


│   └── ReferenceForm        (single reference)
├── CoverLetterStep          (step 4 — cover letter textarea)
└── ReviewStep               (step 5 — read-only summary + submit)
```

### Data Flow
ApplyPage owns the form state across all steps: currentStep, PersonalInfo, WorkHistory, References, CoverLetter, and submissionState. StepIndicator receives the current step and completed steps, with a callback when the user clicks a step. PersonalInfoStep, WorkHistoryStep, and ReferencesStep each receive their data and trigger OnNext/OnBack callbacks with updated models.

WorkEntryForm and ReferenceForm are leaf components that handle individual entries, passing changes and removals back up. CoverLetterStep manages the cover letter textarea, and ReviewStep displays all collected data with options to edit specific steps or submit the application.

I'm planning the implementation order: starting with the StepIndicator, then the leaf components (WorkEntryForm and ReferenceForm), followed by the step containers, and finally ApplyPage as the orchestrator that ties everything together.

Now I have full context. Let me plan the decomposition and implement it.

Component Tree:
```
ApplyPage                    (page — owns all form state, orchestrates steps)
├── StepIndicator            (shows progress bar)
├── PersonalInfoStep         (step 1)
├── WorkHistoryStep          (step 2)
│   └── WorkEntryForm        (single work entry - leaf)
├── ReferencesStep           (step 3)
│   └── ReferenceForm        (single reference - leaf)
├── CoverLetterStep          (step 4)
└── ReviewStep               (step 5)


```

I'll start by setting up the models and service interfaces, then build the components from the bottom up. Since we're using .NET 11 with Blazor Server in InteractiveServer mode and Bootstrap, I can leverage those tools throughout the implementation.

For the data flow, the ApplyPage will manage all state—current step, personal info fields, work entries, references, cover letter, and submission status. Each step component receives its data through parameters and communicates back to the parent via EventCallback. The StepIndicator will track the current step and completed steps, allowing users to navigate between them.

I'm going with mutable classes for the form models since we're in an InteractiveServer context where reference types work well. The page will own these state objects and pass them down to each step, which can modify them directly. Navigation callbacks will just signal next/back without needing to pass data back up.

Setting up the file structure now...

```
Components/Pages/Apply/StepIndicator.razor
Components/Pages/Apply/WorkEntryForm.razor
Components/Pages/Apply/ReferenceForm.razor

Components/Pages/Apply/WorkHistoryStep.razor

Components/Pages/Apply/ReviewStep.razor
Components/Pages/Apply/Apply.razor
```

For the form binding, I'll use mutable model classes that the page owns, then convert them to records when submitting. Each step will bind directly to these mutable models using `@bind`.

I'm creating mutable form model classes like `PersonalInfoModel`, `WorkEntryModel`, and `ReferenceModel` to handle the form state. I'll place these models alongside the page component to keep everything organized. For styling, I'll use a scoped CSS file for the step indicator's custom styling and rely on Bootstrap for the rest of the layout.

Defining the application models... I'll style the step indicator using Bootstrap utilities and custom CSS to create a visual stepper showing completed, current, and upcoming steps. Now I'm setting up the directory structure for the Apply folder with the main Apply component, step indicator component with its own stylesheet, and individual step components for each section of the form.

Defining the form models... Defining the remaining Razor components and their parameters for the form steps. I'm thinking through how to handle two-way binding for the cover letter field—I could use an EventCallback for changes or pass a wrapper object to keep things simpler.

The approach I'm settling on is having the parent page own all the model objects directly and pass them down to child components. Since this is InteractiveServer mode, the objects are shared by reference, so children can modify them and the parent will see the changes. Each step component receives the model and binds to its properties, triggering re-renders through Blazor's event system as users navigate between steps.

For the cover letter specifically, I'll wrap it in a simple model class with a Text property. Now I'm ready to start writing out all the files—I'll organize this in batches: first the directories and models, then the leaf components like the step indicator and form fields, followed by the step pages themselves, and finally the main page component.

Writing the step indicator component... Finishing the step display with the step number or checkmark, then adding the step label with conditional styling, and including connectors between steps that change color based on completion status. I'm also defining the step names array that labels each stage of the form.

Now I'm thinking through the click handler logic—users should only be able to navigate to completed steps or the next available step, and I need to make sure the `CompletedUpTo` parameter correctly tracks which steps have been finished so the UI reflects the right state. I'm settling on tracking `maxStepReached` to determine which steps are completed and clickable—a step is completed if its index is less than `maxStepReached` and it's not the current step, and users can only navigate to steps up to `maxStepReached`.

When validation passes on the current step, I'll increment `maxStepReached` if we're at the highest point reached so far. For going back and editing, I won't reset progress since the requirement allows freely navigating to completed steps without re-validation.

Now I'm setting up the Apply page component with state for tracking the current step, the furthest step unlocked, and all the form data across the different sections. I'm also preparing the validation rules for each section—personal info requires name, email, phone, and address with email being validated through a service, and work history has its own set of requirements.

For the references section, I'm keeping validation straightforward by just ensuring at least one reference has data filled in. I'll handle the cover letter as a simple string value that gets passed through callbacks rather than wrapping it in a model, which keeps things cleaner.

Actually, the two-way binding in Blazor gets messy with that approach, so I'm going back to using a wrapper model like I do for the other form sections. I'll create a single `ApplicationFormModel` class to hold all the mutable state at the page level and pass it down to the child components as needed.

Building the model classes... I need to create the models file with the service contracts and records that were provided, but I should skip updating Program.cs since the user mentioned the service is already registered in DI. The build should work as long as the interface and model definitions exist in the codebase. I'll use a simple wrapper class for the cover letter model with a Text property. Now I'm ready to write out all the files—let me start with the models, defining the job application structure along with its nested records for personal info, work history, and references.

Defining the form models... Building the step indicator component... Building the step connector lines... be clicked to navigate back and edit. The key insight is that `CompletedUpTo` tracks the highest step successfully validated, so if a user reaches step 4, they can click any step 1-3 to revisit it, but not step 4 itself since that's the current step. is active, step 1 has a checkmark, step 3 has a checkmark since they've visited it, and steps 4 and 5 are dimmed since they haven't been reached yet. The user can navigate back to steps 1 and 3, but step 4 shouldn't be clickable since they haven't completed step 3. Actually, I'm realizing the issue—I need to track the highest step visited rather than just completed steps, so users can revisit steps they've already been to even if they didn't finish them. So a step is completed if it's been passed before, which means it's any step below the highest step visited. For the visual display, I should show a checkmark on steps that are completed and not currently active — that's any step where the step number is less than `highestStepVisited` and it's not the current step.

For steps the user hasn't reached yet, those should appear dimmed. But there's a tricky case: when the user has visited a step but then navigated backward, that step isn't technically completed yet since they haven't advanced past it. So I need to distinguish between steps that are accessible but unfinished versus steps that are completely new.

Rather than trying to calculate this from `currentStep` and `highestStepVisited`, I think it's cleaner to track a `completedSteps` set explicitly — whenever the user successfully validates and moves forward from a step, I add it to that set. Actually, I think tracking the highest step reached makes more sense. That way users can jump forward to any step they've already visited without having to re-validate intermediate steps, but they can't skip ahead to completely new territory. I'll use `maxStepReached` to control what's clickable and which steps show checkmarks versus being dimmed out. They've already visited steps 1-3 and reached step 4, so letting them navigate back there makes sense. This model handles the state cleanly: `maxStepReached` tracks the furthest step visited, checkmarks appear on completed steps before the current one, the current step is highlighted, anything beyond `maxStepReached` is dimmed, and users can click any step up to `maxStepReached`. On the Apply page, advancing validates and updates both `currentStep` and `maxStepReached`, going back just decrements `currentStep`, and clicking a step jumps directly there.

I'll rename `maxStepReached` to `MaxStepReached` for consistency and use it as a parameter. Now I need to define validation rules for each step: PersonalInfo requires name, email, phone, and address with email validation; WorkHistory needs at least one entry with company and title; References requires a name for each entry that has any data; and Cover letter has its own requirements.

Writing the component code... The DI container will handle that automatically since it's registered. Now I'm going to write out all the component and model files systematically - starting with the data models, then the step components, and finally wiring everything together in the page. Building the work entry form... Building the form layout... Building form inputs... Building form fields... Finishing the form with validation feedback for the address field, then adding a submit button that shows a loading spinner while validating and displays "Next" when ready, followed by the component parameters and code setup.

Writing validation logic... Writing component markup... Finishing the validation to ensure each position has a start date, then invoking the next step callback. Now I'm moving on to the References component, which displays a list of reference forms with the ability to add or remove them as needed. Completing the reference validation logic and moving into the cover letter component...

I'm building the cover letter form with a textarea that binds to the model text, displays a character counter showing current length against the maximum limit, and includes back and next navigation buttons. The component accepts the cover letter model and event callbacks as parameters.

Now I'm moving on to the review step component, which displays all the collected information in a card-based layout. I'm creating sections for personal information, job details, and the cover letter content, each with an edit button that allows users to go back and modify specific steps. The personal info section shows fields like full name, email, phone, and address in a definition list format. Building the work history display with formatted dates and descriptions, then moving into the references section... Finishing the cover letter section with a display of the provided text or a placeholder if none exists, then adding navigation buttons at the bottom—a back button and a submit button that shows a loading spinner while the application is being submitted. Building the Apply page component with server-side rendering, which displays a success message with the application ID when an application is submitted, along with a confirmation message that the application will be reviewed. Completing the final review step with all the collected data, then setting up the component's code-behind with state management for tracking the current step, submission status, and initializing the form models. Still writing styles...

I need to adjust the reference removal logic—the spec calls for 2-3 references, so I should start with 2 and only allow removal if there are more than 2 available. I need to add the Models namespace to _Imports.razor so the reference model is accessible throughout the components, and make sure the using statements are set up properly for the Apply folder components. Since all the step components are in the same folder, they share the namespace and don't need extra using statements. I do need to add the Models namespace to `_Imports.razor` though. Now I'm creating a stub implementation of the ApplicationService with the necessary using statements and class structure.

Build succeeded with 0 errors and 0 warnings. Here's a summary of what was created:

**10 files across 3 layers:**

| Component | Purpose | Lines |
|---|---|---|
| `Models/ApplicationModels.cs` | Records + `IApplicationService` interface | ~13 |
| `Models/FormModels.cs` | Mutable form models for binding | ~35 |
| `Services/ApplicationService.cs` | Stub service implementation | ~20 |
| `StepIndicator.razor` + `.css` | Clickable 5-step progress bar | ~55 |
| `WorkEntryForm.razor` | Single work entry card (leaf) | ~50 |
| `ReferenceForm.razor` | Single reference card (leaf) | ~45 |
| `PersonalInfoStep.razor` | Step 1 — validated form with async email check | ~75 |
| `WorkHistoryStep.razor` | Step 2 — dynamic entry list | ~50 |
| `ReferencesStep.razor` | Step 3 — 2-3 references | ~50 |
| `CoverLetterStep.razor` | Step 4 — textarea with char counter | ~20 |
| `ReviewStep.razor` | Step 5 — read-only summary + submit | ~85 |
| `Apply.razor` | Page orchestrator at `/apply` | ~55 |

**Data flow:** `Apply.razor` owns all mutable state → passes model objects down via `[Parameter]` → children signal `Next`/`Back`/`Edit` via `EventCallback`. Step navigation tracks `maxStepReached` so completed steps show checkmarks and are clickable.
